Toronto After Dark Film Festival

**Toronto After Dark Film Festival** (TADFF) is a showcase of [horror](/wiki/Horror_film "Horror film"), [sci-fi](/wiki/Science_fiction_film "Science fiction film"), [action](/wiki/Action_film "Action film") and [cult cinema](/wiki/Cult_film "Cult film") held annually in Toronto, Ontario, Canada. The festival premieres a diverse selection of feature-length and short-films from around the world including new works from Asia, Europe and North America. The festival was founded by Adam Lopez in 2006.[[1]](#cite_note-Lopez-1)

## Past and upcoming festivals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto_After_Dark_Film_Festival&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Past and upcoming festivals")]

### 2006

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto_After_Dark_Film_Festival&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 2006")]

The 1st Edition of the Toronto After Dark Film Festival took place October 20–24, 2006. The inaugural festival was held at the [Bloor Cinema](/wiki/Bloor_Cinema "Bloor Cinema") in [Toronto, Ontario](/wiki/Toronto,_Ontario "Toronto, Ontario"), Canada.

The 2006 Toronto After Dark Film Festival Official Feature Film Selections include 13 films from around the world, and 15 short films from Canada. Some of the films screened at the festival included *[Special](/wiki/Special_(film) "Special (film)")*, directed by [Hal Haberman](/w/index.php?title=Hal_Haberman&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hal Haberman (page does not exist)") and [Jeremy Passmorewhere](/w/index.php?title=Jeremy_Passmorewhere&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jeremy Passmorewhere (page does not exist)"), the [J-horror](/wiki/J-horror "J-horror") film *[Retribution](/wiki/Retribution_(2006_film) "Retribution (2006 film)")* from cult director [Kiyoshi Kurosawa](/wiki/Kiyoshi_Kurosawa "Kiyoshi Kurosawa"), the North American premiere of the Thai action-horror film *[Vengeance](/w/index.php?title=Vengeance_(2006_film)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Vengeance (2006 film) (page does not exist)")*, and *[Behind the Mask: The Rise of Leslie Vernon](/wiki/Behind_the_Mask:_The_Rise_of_Leslie_Vernon "Behind the Mask: The Rise of Leslie Vernon")*, which won the festival's first Audience Award.

Other films included *[The Beach Party at the Threshold of Hell](/wiki/The_Beach_Party_at_the_Threshold_of_Hell "The Beach Party at the Threshold of Hell")*, *[Shinobi: Heart Under Blade](/wiki/Shinobi:_Heart_Under_Blade "Shinobi: Heart Under Blade")*, *[Night of the Living Dorks](/wiki/Night_of_the_Living_Dorks "Night of the Living Dorks")*, *[Re-cycle](/wiki/Re-cycle "Re-cycle")*, *[Mad Cowgirl](/wiki/Mad_Cowgirl "Mad Cowgirl")*, *[Slaughter Night](/wiki/Slaughter_Night "Slaughter Night")*, and *[Naisu no mori: The First Contact](/wiki/Naisu_no_mori:_The_First_Contact "Naisu no mori: The First Contact")*. The festival also featured a "Make Your Own Damn Movie!" masterclass taught by [Lloyd Kaufman](/wiki/Lloyd_Kaufman "Lloyd Kaufman"). Festival attendance was 4,350.

### 2007

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto_After_Dark_Film_Festival&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 2007")]

The 2nd Edition of the Toronto After Dark Film Festival took place October 19–25, 2007.

The second annual festival was held from October 19–25, 2007, at the Bloor Cinema. The festival expanded to fifty films: fourteen features and 36 short films from around the world, including 15 Canadian shorts. Festival highlights included *[The Tripper](/wiki/The_Tripper "The Tripper")*, directed by [David Arquette](/wiki/David_Arquette "David Arquette"); director [Shinya Tsukamoto](/wiki/Shinya_Tsukamoto "Shinya Tsukamoto")'s *[Nightmare Detective](/wiki/Nightmare_Detective "Nightmare Detective")*; the premiere of [Troma Entertainment](/wiki/Troma_Entertainment "Troma Entertainment")'s *[Poultrygeist: Night of the Chicken Dead](/wiki/Poultrygeist:_Night_of_the_Chicken_Dead "Poultrygeist: Night of the Chicken Dead")*; [Uwe Boll](/wiki/Uwe_Boll "Uwe Boll")'s *[In the Name of the King: A Dungeon Siege Tale](/wiki/In_the_Name_of_the_King:_A_Dungeon_Siege_Tale "In the Name of the King: A Dungeon Siege Tale")*; the zombie-western short film *[It Came From The West](/w/index.php?title=It_Came_From_The_West&action=edit&redlink=1 "It Came From The West (page does not exist)")*; the world premiere of [John Bergin](/wiki/John_Bergin "John Bergin")'s *[From Inside](/w/index.php?title=From_Inside&action=edit&redlink=1 "From Inside (page does not exist)")*; *[Mulberry Street](/wiki/Mulberry_Street_(film) "Mulberry Street (film)")*, which went on to win the festival's After Dark Spirit Award; and the Thai horror film *[Alone](/wiki/Alone_(2007_film) "Alone (2007 film)")* from directors [Banjong Pisanthanakun](/wiki/Banjong_Pisanthanakun "Banjong Pisanthanakun") and [Parkpoom Wongpoom](/wiki/Parkpoom_Wongpoom "Parkpoom Wongpoom"), which won the festival's annual Audience Award. Festival attendance was 6,500.

Other films included *[Blood Car](/wiki/Blood_Car "Blood Car")*, *[Wolfhound](/wiki/Volkodav_from_the_Grey_Hound_Clan_(film) "Volkodav from the Grey Hound Clan (film)")*, *[The Rebel](/wiki/The_Rebel_(2007_Vietnamese_film) "The Rebel (2007 Vietnamese film)")*, *[Audience of One](/wiki/Audience_of_One_(film) "Audience of One (film)")*, *[Aachi & Ssipak](/wiki/Aachi_%26_Ssipak "Aachi & Ssipak")*, *[Ambassador's Day](/w/index.php?title=Ambassador%27s_Day&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ambassador's Day (page does not exist)")*, *[Murder Party](/wiki/Murder_Party "Murder Party")*, *[Operation Fish](/wiki/Operation_Fish "Operation Fish")*, *[Terror On The 3918](/w/index.php?title=Terror_On_The_3918&action=edit&redlink=1 "Terror On The 3918 (page does not exist)")*,[[2]](#cite_note-2) *[Simon Says](/wiki/Simon_Says_(film) "Simon Says (film)")*, among others.

### 2008

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto_After_Dark_Film_Festival&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2008")]

The 3rd Edition of the Toronto After Dark Film Festival took place October 17–24, 2008.

The third annual festival expanded to eight nights and was held from October 17–24, 2008, at the Bloor Cinema. Highlights of the third Annual Festival included sell-out screenings of *[Let the Right One In](/wiki/Let_the_Right_One_In_(film) "Let the Right One In (film)")*, *[Repo! The Genetic Opera](/wiki/Repo!_The_Genetic_Opera "Repo! The Genetic Opera")*, *[Tokyo Gore Police](/wiki/Tokyo_Gore_Police "Tokyo Gore Police")*, and the North American Premiere of *[I Sell The Dead](/wiki/I_Sell_The_Dead "I Sell The Dead")*; over 30 attending filmmakers on stage to introduce their films, including [Larry Fessenden](/wiki/Larry_Fessenden "Larry Fessenden") (*The Last Winter*) and [Angus Scrimm](/wiki/Angus_Scrimm "Angus Scrimm") (*Phantasm*); packed nightly social events; positive reviews for the films programmed; and a *[Zombie Walk](/wiki/Zombie_Walk "Zombie Walk")*. Other films included *[Trailer Park of Terror](/wiki/Trailer_Park_of_Terror "Trailer Park of Terror")*, Mirageman, *[Idiots and Angels](/wiki/Idiots_and_Angels "Idiots and Angels")*, *[Who is KK Downey?](/wiki/Who_is_KK_Downey%3F "Who is KK Downey?")*, *[4bia](/wiki/4bia "4bia")*, *Brain Dead*, *[Donkey Punch](/wiki/Donkey_Punch_(2008_film) "Donkey Punch (2008 film)")*, *[Mutant Chronicles](/wiki/Mutant_Chronicles_(film) "Mutant Chronicles (film)")*, and *[Home Movie](/wiki/Home_Movie_(2008_film) "Home Movie (2008 film)")*. The festival also featured a Make Your Own Damn Movie! masterclass taught by [Lloyd Kaufman](/wiki/Lloyd_Kaufman "Lloyd Kaufman"). Festival attendance was over 8,500.

Over 3,500 votes were cast for the Audience Award for Best Feature Film and the Gold winner was Swedish vampire film *[Let the Right One In](/wiki/Let_the_Right_One_In_(film) "Let the Right One In (film)")*. Silver went to cult sci-fi horror musical *[Repo! The Genetic Opera](/wiki/Repo!_The_Genetic_Opera "Repo! The Genetic Opera")*, and Bronze was awarded by fans to the Thai supernatural anthology movie *[4bia](/wiki/4bia "4bia")* (*Phobia*). Awards were also handed out for Best Independent Feature Film, where the big winner was the grave-robber comedy, *[I Sell The Dead](/wiki/I_Sell_The_Dead "I Sell The Dead")*, and in the short film categories, winners included the Christmas tree exploitation film *Treevenge*.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

### 2009

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto_After_Dark_Film_Festival&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2009")]

The 4th Edition of the Toronto After Dark Film Festival took place August 14–21, 2009.

The fourth annual festival made the move to summer, once again at the Bloor Cinema with over 9,000 fans attending the festival. Highlights included screenings of the Norwegian Nazi zombie horror-comedy *[Dead Snow](/wiki/Dead_Snow "Dead Snow")*, the horror anthology *[Trick 'r Treat](/wiki/Trick_%27r_Treat "Trick 'r Treat")* and the cult [blaxploitation](/wiki/Blaxploitation "Blaxploitation") action-comedy *[Black Dynamite](/wiki/Black_Dynamite "Black Dynamite")*, which won Gold, Silver and Bronze respectively in the Audience Choice Awards. The festival also hosted its first ever world premiere, *[Strigoi](/wiki/Strigoi_(film) "Strigoi (film)")*, an offbeat Romanian vampire comedy that was awarded by the fest Gold in the Best Independent Feature Film category. Silver and Bronze honours went to the dark comedy *[The Revenant](/wiki/The_Revenant_(2009_film) "The Revenant (2009 film)")* and the zombie thriller, *[Grace](/wiki/Grace_(2009_film) "Grace (2009 film)")*, respectively.

Other films included *[Vampire Girl vs. Frankenstein Girl](/wiki/Vampire_Girl_vs._Frankenstein_Girl "Vampire Girl vs. Frankenstein Girl")*, *[The Warlords](/wiki/The_Warlords "The Warlords")* (starring Jet Li, Andy Lau and Takeshi Kaneshiro), *[Black](/wiki/Black_(Film_2008) "Black (Film 2008)")* (starring MC Jean Gab'1), *Rough Cut*,[[4]](#cite_note-4) *[Franklyn](/wiki/Franklyn "Franklyn")* (starring Ryan Phillippe and Eva Green), *The Forbidden Door*,[[5]](#cite_note-5) *Must Love Death*,[[6]](#cite_note-6) *[Someone's Knocking at the Door](/wiki/Someone%27s_Knocking_at_the_Door "Someone's Knocking at the Door")*,[[7]](#cite_note-7) *[The Children](/wiki/The_Children_(2008_film) "The Children (2008 film)")*, *[Embodiment of Evil](/wiki/Embodiment_of_Evil "Embodiment of Evil")* (the third film in the *[Coffin Joe](/wiki/Coffin_Joe "Coffin Joe")* trilogy), and *[The Dark Hour](/wiki/The_Dark_Hour_(2007_film) "The Dark Hour (2007 film)")*. *Shorts After Dark (International Short Film Snowcase)* comprised eleven new horror, sci-fi, action, and cult short films from around the world, including a number of international award winners.[[8]](#cite_note-8) *Canada After Dark (Canadian Short Film Snowcase)* featured 17 "home-grown" short films (and are the opening acts for the feature films).[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### 2010

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto_After_Dark_Film_Festival&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2010")]

The 5th Edition of the Toronto After Dark Film Festival took place August 13–20, 2010.

The fifth annual festival,[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) once again at the Bloor Cinema with over 9,300 fans attending. Highlights included the festival's first-ever red carpet premiere for *[The Last Exorcism](/wiki/The_Last_Exorcism "The Last Exorcism")*, with producer Eli Roth, stars Ashley Bell and Patrick Fabian attending, the horror film *[The Human Centipede](/wiki/The_Human_Centipede "The Human Centipede")*, and the 2010 remake of the 1978 exploitation film *[I Spit On Your Grave](/wiki/I_Spit_on_Your_Grave_(2010_film) "I Spit on Your Grave (2010 film)")*. These three films won Gold, Silver and Bronze respectively, in the Audience Choice Awards.

Other films included *[The Last Lovecraft: Relic of Cthulhu](/wiki/The_Last_Lovecraft:_Relic_of_Cthulhu "The Last Lovecraft: Relic of Cthulhu")*, *[Doghouse](/wiki/Doghouse_(film) "Doghouse (film)")*, *[Evil In the Time of Heroes](/wiki/Evil_-_In_the_Time_of_Heroes "Evil - In the Time of Heroes")*, *[Robogeisha](/wiki/RoboGeisha "RoboGeisha")*, *[Alien vs Ninja](/wiki/Alien_vs_Ninja "Alien vs Ninja")*, *[Phobia 2](/wiki/Phobia_2 "Phobia 2")*, *[Cargo](/wiki/Cargo_(2009_film) "Cargo (2009 film)")*, *[Centurion](/wiki/Centurion_(film) "Centurion (film)")*, *[Black Death](/wiki/Black_Death_(film) "Black Death (film)")*, *[All About Evil](/wiki/All_About_Evil "All About Evil")*, *[High School](/wiki/High_School_(2010_film) "High School (2010 film)")* and *[Rubber](/wiki/Rubber_(2010_film) "Rubber (2010 film)")*.

*Shorts After Dark (International Short Film Snowcase)* comprised 10 new horror, sci-fi, action, and cult short films from around the world, including a number of international award winners and premieres.[[12]](#cite_note-12) 2010's Best International Short Film award winners included *Off Season* (Gold), *[Pumzi](/wiki/Pumzi "Pumzi")* (Silver), and *Deus Irae* (Bronze). *Canada After Dark (Canadian Short Film Snowcase)* featured 16 "home-grown" short films (and are the opening acts for the feature films).[[13]](#cite_note-13) 2010's Best Canadian Short Film award winners included *Junko's Shamisen* (Gold),[[14]](#cite_note-14) *King Chicken* (Silver), and *Fireman* and *Game Night* (tied for Bronze).

### 2011

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto_After_Dark_Film_Festival&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2011")]

The 6th Edition of the Toronto After Dark Film Festival took place October 20–27, 2011.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Monster_Brawl_cast_and_crew.jpg/250px-Monster_Brawl_cast_and_crew.jpg)](/wiki/File:Monster_Brawl_cast_and_crew.jpg)

Cast and crew of the film *Monster Brawl* at the gala opening of the 2011 festival.

The sixth annual festival returned to October and the Halloween season for the first time in three years, at the Toronto Underground Cinema. A record number of feature films and short film submissions were received this year.

Toronto After Dark announced the first eight Official Selections of its 2011 film line-up at [Fan Expo Canada](/wiki/Fan_Expo_Canada "Fan Expo Canada") on August 25, 2011, then on its official website on August 31, 2011.[[15]](#cite_note-15) The films selected included *[Monster Brawl](/wiki/Monster_Brawl "Monster Brawl")*; *Exit Humanity*, a zombie saga set against the back-drop of the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"); the Canadian Premiere of the Pierce Brothers' *[DeadHeads](/wiki/Deadheads_(film) "Deadheads (film)")*, a multi-genre zombie buddy film; *[Redline](/wiki/Redline_(2009_film) "Redline (2009 film)")*, a Japanese [anime](/wiki/Anime "Anime") set in the world of car racing; *[The Theatre Bizarre](/wiki/The_Theatre_Bizarre "The Theatre Bizarre")*, a horror anthology from such genre filmmakers as [Tom Savini](/wiki/Tom_Savini "Tom Savini") and [Richard Stanley](/wiki/Richard_Stanley_(film_director) "Richard Stanley (film director)"); the World Premiere of *Father's Day*, a exploitation/grindhouse story from [Astron-6](/wiki/Astron-6 "Astron-6") and [Troma](/wiki/Troma "Troma"); *[Love](/wiki/Love_(2011_film) "Love (2011 film)")*, a time-travel sci-fi drama; and [Xavier Gens](/wiki/Xavier_Gens "Xavier Gens")'s *[The Divide](/wiki/The_Divide_(2011_film) "The Divide (2011 film)")*, an apocalyptic film about the residents of a New York City apartment building.

Toronto After Dark was honoured in hosting two more World Premiere screenings. The WWII action-horror-zombie film, *[War of the Dead](/wiki/War_of_the_Dead "War of the Dead")*, directed by [Marko Mäkilaakso](/wiki/Marko_M%C3%A4kilaakso "Marko Mäkilaakso") and the dark, superhero thriller, *[VS](/wiki/Vs_(film) "Vs (film)")*, directed by Jason Trost (co-writer, director, and star of *THE FP*) and starring [James Remar](/wiki/James_Remar "James Remar").

Other films include the *Jack Perez*'s horror comedy, *Some Guy Who Kills People*, starring [Kevin Corrigan](/wiki/Kevin_Corrigan "Kevin Corrigan"), the vampire-drama, *[Midnight Son](/wiki/Midnight_Son "Midnight Son")*, the award-winning supernatural thriller, *[Absentia](/wiki/Absentia_(film) "Absentia (film)")*, the action-thriller, *[A Lonely Place to Die](/wiki/A_Lonely_Place_to_Die "A Lonely Place to Die")*, [Astron-6](/wiki/Astron-6 "Astron-6")'s sci-fi, action-comedy, *[Manborg](/wiki/Manborg "Manborg")*, the Canadian sci-fi thriller, *The Corridor*, [Lucky McKee](/wiki/Lucky_McKee "Lucky McKee")'s controversial film *[The Woman](/wiki/The_Woman_(2011_film) "The Woman (2011 film)")*, and [Ti West](/wiki/Ti_West "Ti West")'s haunted hotel thriller, *[The Innkeepers](/wiki/The_Innkeepers_(film) "The Innkeepers (film)")*.

[Astron-6](/wiki/Astron-6 "Astron-6")'s *Father's Day*, *[The Divide](/wiki/The_Divide_(2011_film) "The Divide (2011 film)")* and *[DeadHeads](/wiki/Deadheads_(film) "Deadheads (film)")* won Gold, Silver and Bronze respectively, in the Audience Choice Awards for Best Feature Film.

### 2012

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto_After_Dark_Film_Festival&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2012")]

The 7th Edition of the Toronto After Dark Film Festival took place October 18–26, 2012.

The seventh annual festival saw the Toronto After Dark Film Festival return to the Bloor Cinema, which went through new ownership and a complete renovation, reopening as the, [Hot Docs Ted Rogers Cinema](/wiki/Hot_Docs_Ted_Rogers_Cinema "Hot Docs Ted Rogers Cinema"). The festival also expanded one extra night, showcasing a total of twenty feature films and a wealth of Canadian and International short films. The Toronto After Dark Film Festival hosted two *Spotlight Presentation* Screening Nights Summer 2012, taking place also at Bloor Cinema, Wednesday, June 27 and Wednesday, July 11. These two evenings showcased two films each night, including *[Juan of the Dead](/wiki/Juan_of_the_Dead "Juan of the Dead")*, *[The Pact](/wiki/The_Pact_(2012_film) "The Pact (2012 film)")*, *[Detention](/wiki/Detention_(2011_film) "Detention (2011 film)")* and the Canadian Premiere of *[V/H/S](/wiki/V/H/S "V/H/S")*.

The 2012 Toronto After Dark Film Festival Official Selections included the Canadian Premiere of the body-modification thriller *[American Mary](/wiki/American_Mary "American Mary")*, from Vancouver-based writers, producers and directors, Sylvia and Jen Soska (otherwise known as the [Soska sisters](/wiki/Soska_sisters "Soska sisters")). Other selections included, the Irish alien-monster horror-comedy *[Grabbers](/wiki/Grabbers "Grabbers")*; the dark crime-thriller *[Crave](/wiki/Crave_(film) "Crave (film)")*, directed by DVD and Blu-ray producer and filmmaker, [Charles de Lauzirika](/wiki/Charles_de_Lauzirika "Charles de Lauzirika"); the British horror film *[Inbred](/wiki/Inbred_(film) "Inbred (film)")*; the third installment of the *[REC series](/wiki/REC_(film) "REC (film)")*, *[REC 3: Génesis](/wiki/REC_3:_G%C3%A9nesis "REC 3: Génesis")*; the British zombie-comedy *[Cockneys vs Zombies](/wiki/Cockneys_vs_Zombies "Cockneys vs Zombies")*; *[Honor Blackman](/wiki/Honor_Blackman "Honor Blackman")*; the South Korean science-fiction anthology film *[Doomsday Book](/wiki/Doomsday_Book_(film) "Doomsday Book (film)")*; the Canadian *["LARP"-ing](/wiki/LARP "LARP")* comedy *[Lloyd The Conqueror](/wiki/Lloyd_the_Conqueror "Lloyd the Conqueror")*; the latest UniSol installment, *[Universal Soldier: Day of Reckoning](/wiki/Universal_Soldier:_Day_of_Reckoning "Universal Soldier: Day of Reckoning")*; the dark fantasy-thriller *[After](/wiki/After_(2012_film) "After (2012 film)")*; the paranormal sequel *[Grave Encounters 2](/wiki/Grave_Encounters_2 "Grave Encounters 2")*; the Irish psychological-horror film *[Citadel](/wiki/Citadel_(film) "Citadel (film)")*, from director, [Ciaran Foy](/wiki/Ciaran_Foy "Ciaran Foy"); the documentary *My Amityville Horror*, in which Daniel Lutz, one of the children who lived through *[The Amityville Horror](/wiki/Amityville_Horror "Amityville Horror")*, is interviewed; *[Resolution](/wiki/Resolution_(film) "Resolution (film)")*, directed by Justin Benson and Aaron Moorhead; *[Tony Todd](/wiki/Tony_Todd "Tony Todd")*, *[Mark Hamill](/wiki/Mark_Hamill "Mark Hamill")*, *[Noah Hathaway](/wiki/Noah_Hathaway "Noah Hathaway")*, *[James Duval](/wiki/James_Duval "James Duval")* and newcomer, [Cortney Palm](/wiki/Cortney_Palm "Cortney Palm"), highlight Kern Saxton's revenge crime-thriller, *[Sushi Girl](/wiki/Sushi_Girl "Sushi Girl")*; Japanese director, [Noboru Iguchi](/wiki/Noboru_Iguchi "Noboru Iguchi")'s comedy-horror, *[Dead Sushi](/wiki/Dead_Sushi "Dead Sushi")*; the home-invasion thriller *[In Their Skin](/wiki/In_their_skin "In their skin")*; and *[Wrong](/wiki/Wrong_(film) "Wrong (film)")*, the latest film from *[Quentin Dupieux (aka Mr. Oizo)](/wiki/Mr._Oizo "Mr. Oizo")*.

*[A Fantastic Fear of Everything](/wiki/A_Fantastic_Fear_of_Everything "A Fantastic Fear of Everything")*, starring *[Simon Pegg](/wiki/Simon_Pegg "Simon Pegg")*, and Juan Martínez Moreno's Spanish werewolf-comedy, *[Game of Werewolves (Lobos de Arga)](/wiki/Game_of_Werewolves "Game of Werewolves")*, rounded out the closing night of the festival.

*[Cockneys vs Zombies](/wiki/Cockneys_vs_Zombies "Cockneys vs Zombies")*, *[Dead Sushi](/wiki/Dead_Sushi "Dead Sushi")* and *[A Fantastic Fear of Everything](/wiki/A_Fantastic_Fear_of_Everything "A Fantastic Fear of Everything")* won Gold, Silver and Bronze respectively, in the Audience Choice Awards for Best Feature Film.

### 2013

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto_After_Dark_Film_Festival&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2013")]

The 8th Edition of the Toronto After Dark Film Festival took place October 17–25, 2013.

The eighth annual edition of the Toronto After Dark Film Festival saw the festival partner with Canada's Cineplex Entertainment, for the first time, with the [Scotiabank Theatre](/wiki/Scotiabank_Theatre_Toronto "Scotiabank Theatre Toronto") being the venue host for the festival.

The Toronto After Dark Film Festival hosted two pre-festival Screening Nights in the summer of 2013, also taking place at the Scotiabank Theatre, Thursday, August 29 and Thursday, September 26. These two evenings screened two films each night. They included, *[Bad Milo](/wiki/Bad_Milo "Bad Milo")*; *[V/H/S/2](/wiki/V/H/S/2 "V/H/S/2")*; the Canadian Premiere of *[Europa Report](/wiki/Europa_Report "Europa Report")* and, *[The Dirties](/wiki/The_Dirties "The Dirties")*.

The 2013 Toronto After Dark Film Festival Official Feature Film Selections include the Opening Gala film and Toronto Premiere of [Jim Mickle](/wiki/Jim_Mickle "Jim Mickle")'s *[We Are What We Are](/wiki/We_Are_What_We_Are_(2013_film) "We Are What We Are (2013 film)")*. This marked a return to Toronto After Dark for Mickle, as his 2006 film, *[Mulberry Street](/wiki/Mulberry_Street_(film) "Mulberry Street (film)")* was a previous Toronto After Dark Official Selection. Other returning Toronto After Dark alumni for 2013 included Henry Saine with *[Bounty Killer](/wiki/Bounty_Killer_(film) "Bounty Killer (film)")* and [Jesse Thomas Cook](/wiki/Jesse_Thomas_Cook "Jesse Thomas Cook") with *[Septic Man](/wiki/Septic_Man "Septic Man")* for the Canadian Premiere. Other selections included creature features *[Big Ass Spider!](/wiki/Big_Ass_Spider! "Big Ass Spider!")* and *[Eega](/wiki/Eega "Eega")*; the British zombie-comedy *[Stalled](/wiki/Stalled "Stalled")*; the zombie-drama *[The Battery](/wiki/The_Battery_(film) "The Battery (film)")*, directed by [Jeremy Gardner](/wiki/Jeremy_Gardner_(actor) "Jeremy Gardner (actor)"); the World Premiere of the Canadian thriller *Silent Retreat*, directed by *Tricia Lee*; Don Thacker's *Motivational Growth*; the Canadian Premiere of *[Odd Thomas](/wiki/Odd_Thomas_(film) "Odd Thomas (film)")*, directed by [Stephen Sommers](/wiki/Stephen_Sommers "Stephen Sommers") based on the novels by [Dean Koontz](/wiki/Dean_Koontz "Dean Koontz"); the Canadian horror-thriller *[Solo](/wiki/Solo_(2013_film) "Solo (2013 film)")*; the sci-fi films *[The Last Days on Mars](/wiki/Last_Days_on_Mars "Last Days on Mars")* and *[The Machine](/wiki/The_Machine_(2013_film) "The Machine (2013 film)")*, directed by Caradog W. James; the Toronto premiere of *Found*, directed by Scott Schrimer; the World Premiere of the Canadian action-horror-dark comedy film *Evil Feed*, directed by Kimani Ray Smith; the Bigfoot found-footage thriller *[Willow Creek](/wiki/Willow_Creek_(film) "Willow Creek (film)")*, directed by [Bobcat Goldthwait](/wiki/Bobcat_Goldthwait "Bobcat Goldthwait"); and Blair Erickson's *[Banshee Chapter](/wiki/Banshee_Chapter "Banshee Chapter")*.

*[Cheap Thrills](/wiki/Cheap_Thrills_(film) "Cheap Thrills (film)")* rounded out the closing night of the festival along with *[Big Bad Wolves](/wiki/Big_Bad_Wolves "Big Bad Wolves")*, a dark crime-thriller from Israel directed by [Aharon Keshales](/wiki/Aharon_Keshales "Aharon Keshales") and [Navot Papushado](/wiki/Navot_Papushado "Navot Papushado"), selected as the Closing Gala film; [Quentin Tarantino](/wiki/Quentin_Tarantino "Quentin Tarantino") called it the best film of 2013.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

*The Battery*, *Solo* and *Banshee Chapter* won Gold, Silver and Bronze respectively in the Audience Choice Awards for Best Feature Film.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### 2014

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto_After_Dark_Film_Festival&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 2014")]

The 9th Edition of the Toronto After Dark Film Festival took place October 17–25, 2014.

The ninth annual edition of the Toronto After Dark Film Festival continued to partner with Canada's Cineplex Entertainment, with their Scotiabank Theatre location in downtown Toronto, the venue partner.

The 2014 Toronto After Dark Film Festival Official Feature Film Selections include, the Opening Gala film and Toronto premiere of Gerard Johnstone's *[Housebound](/wiki/Housebound_(2014_film) "Housebound (2014 film)")*. Other selections included *[Suburban Gothic](/wiki/Suburban_Gothic_(film) "Suburban Gothic (film)")*, directed by Richard Bates Jr. (*[Excision](/wiki/Excision_(film) "Excision (film)")*) and starring [Matthew Gray Gubler](/wiki/Matthew_Gray_Gubler "Matthew Gray Gubler"), [Kat Dennings](/wiki/Kat_Dennings "Kat Dennings") and [Ray Wise](/wiki/Ray_Wise "Ray Wise"); the World premiere of the Canadian film, *[Hellmouth](/wiki/Hellmouth_(film) "Hellmouth (film)")*, directed by Toronto After Dark Alumni, John Geddes (*Exit Humanity*), written by [Tony Burgess](/wiki/Tony_Burgess_(author) "Tony Burgess (author)") (*[Pontypool](/wiki/Pontypool_(film) "Pontypool (film)")*) and starring [Stephen McHattie](/wiki/Stephen_McHattie "Stephen McHattie"); the Canadian premiere of *[ABCs of Death 2](/wiki/ABCs_of_Death_2 "ABCs of Death 2")*, directed by several Toronto After Dark Alumni, such as [Jen Soska and Sylvia Soska](/wiki/Soska_sisters "Soska sisters") (*[American Mary](/wiki/American_Mary "American Mary")*), [Julian Gilbey](/wiki/Julian_Gilbey "Julian Gilbey") (*[A Lonely Place to Die](/wiki/A_Lonely_Place_to_Die "A Lonely Place to Die")*), Steven Kostanski (*[Manborg](/wiki/Manborg "Manborg")*) and Juan Martínez Moreno (*[Game of Werewolves](/wiki/Lobos_de_Arga "Lobos de Arga")*); *[Zombeavers](/wiki/Zombeavers "Zombeavers")*, who in turn are trying to eat Toronto After Dark alumni actress, [Cortney Palm](/wiki/Cortney_Palm "Cortney Palm") (*[Sushi Girl](/wiki/Sushi_Girl "Sushi Girl")*); Nazi zombies return in the sequel *[Dead Snow 2: Red vs. Dead](/wiki/Dead_Snow:_Red_vs._Dead "Dead Snow: Red vs. Dead")*, directed by Toronto After Dark alumni, [Tommy Wirkola](/wiki/Tommy_Wirkola "Tommy Wirkola"); the Canadian 'splasher' film *[The Drownsman](/wiki/The_Drownsman "The Drownsman")*, directed by [Chad Archibald](/wiki/Chad_Archibald "Chad Archibald") (*Neverlost*, *Antisocial* and *[Ejecta](/wiki/Ejecta_(film) "Ejecta (film)")*); the North American Premiere of *[Wolves](/wiki/Wolves_(2014_film) "Wolves (2014 film)")*, directed by [David Hayter](/wiki/David_Hayter "David Hayter") (best known as the voice of Solid Snake from the *[Metal Gear Solid](/wiki/Metal_Gear_Solid "Metal Gear Solid")* video games); *[Late Phases](/wiki/Late_Phases "Late Phases")*, directed by Adrián García Bogliano (*[Here Comes the Devil (Ahí va el diablo)](/wiki/Here_Comes_the_Devil "Here Comes the Devil")*), and starring [Nick Damici](/wiki/Nick_Damici "Nick Damici"), [Ethan Embry](/wiki/Ethan_Embry "Ethan Embry") and [Lance Guest](/wiki/Lance_Guest "Lance Guest"); [Elijah Wood](/wiki/Elijah_Wood "Elijah Wood") and [Sasha Grey](/wiki/Sasha_Grey "Sasha Grey") star in *[Open Windows](/wiki/Open_Windows_(film) "Open Windows (film)")*, directed by [Nacho Vigalondo](/wiki/Nacho_Vigalondo "Nacho Vigalondo") (*[Timecrimes (Los cronocrímenes)](/wiki/Timecrimes "Timecrimes")*); [Rinko Kikuchi](/wiki/Rinko_Kikuchi "Rinko Kikuchi") stars in *[Kumiko, the Treasure Hunter](/wiki/Kumiko,_the_Treasure_Hunter "Kumiko, the Treasure Hunter")*; *[Predestination](/wiki/Predestination_(film) "Predestination (film)")*, directed by [Michael Spierig and Peter Spierig](/wiki/Spierig_brothers "Spierig brothers") and starring *[Ethan Hawke](/wiki/Ethan_Hawke "Ethan Hawke")*; Bradley King's *[Time Lapse](/wiki/Time_Lapse_(film) "Time Lapse (film)")*, starring [Danielle Panabaker](/wiki/Danielle_Panabaker "Danielle Panabaker"); the Canadian premiere of *[Refuge](/w/index.php?title=Refuge_(2013_film)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Refuge (2013 film) (page does not exist)")*, directed by Andrew Robertson; the Canadian premiere of the Australian zombie-action-comedy *[Wyrmwood](/wiki/Wyrmwood "Wyrmwood")*, directed by Kiah Roache-Turner; the Canadian premiere of *[The Town That Dreaded Sundown](/wiki/The_Town_That_Dreaded_Sundown_(2014_film) "The Town That Dreaded Sundown (2014 film)")*, a meta-sequel of the 1976 film, directed by [Alfonso Gomez-Rejon](/wiki/Alfonso_Gomez-Rejon "Alfonso Gomez-Rejon"); and the Canadian premiere of the documentary *[Why Horror?](/wiki/Why_Horror%3F "Why Horror?")*

The UK film *Let Us Prey*, starring *[The Woman](/wiki/The_Woman_(2011_film) "The Woman (2011 film)")*'s [Pollyanna McIntosh](/wiki/Pollyanna_McIntosh "Pollyanna McIntosh") rounded out the closing night of the festival, along with *[The Babadook](/wiki/The_Babadook "The Babadook")*, a creepy psychological-horror from Australia directed by [Jennifer Kent](/wiki/Jennifer_Kent "Jennifer Kent"), selected as the Closing Gala film.

*[Dead Snow 2: Red vs. Dead](/wiki/Dead_Snow:_Red_vs._Dead "Dead Snow: Red vs. Dead")*, *[Predestination](/wiki/Predestination_(film) "Predestination (film)")* and *[Why Horror?](/wiki/Why_Horror%3F "Why Horror?")* won Gold, Silver and Bronze respectively in the Audience Choice Awards for Best Feature Film.[[18]](#cite_note-torontoafterdark.com-18)

The Canadian short films screened during the festival include, *Foxed!*, *Young Blood*, *Pupa*, *Lumberjacked*, *Day 40*, *Period Piece*, *Kismet*, *Rose In Bloom*, *Dead Hearts*, *The Monitor*, *Migration*, *What Doesn't Kill You*, *Honor Code*, *Monster Island*, *Lazy Boyz*, *Satan's Dolls*, *Last Breath*, *Little Matthew* and *Intruders*.

*Period Piece*, *Dead Hearts* and *Satan's Dolls* won Gold, Silver and Bronze respectively in the Audience Choice Awards for Best Canadian Short Film.[[18]](#cite_note-torontoafterdark.com-18)

The International short films screened during the festival include, *Invaders*, *Everything and Everything and Everything*, *Sword fights*, *Happy B-Day*, *Strange Thing*, *He Took Off His Skin For Me*, *Liquid*, *Redaction* and *Dynamic Venus*.

*Invaders*, *Happy B-Day* and *He Took Off His Skin For Me* won Gold, Silver and Bronze respectively in the Audience Choice Awards for Best International Short Film.[[18]](#cite_note-torontoafterdark.com-18)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto_After_Dark_Film_Festival&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: 2019")]

The 14th Edition of the Toronto After Dark Film Festival took place October 17–25, 2019.

The 2019 Toronto After Dark Film Festival Official Feature Film Selections include the Irish supernatural comedy, *[Extra Ordinary](/wiki/Extra_Ordinary_(film) "Extra Ordinary (film)")* (as the Opening Night Gala film); *Witches in the Woods*; the science-fiction film, *Blood Machines*, directed by Seth Ickerman and scored by [Carpenter Brut](/wiki/Carpenter_Brut "Carpenter Brut"), based upon the successful Kickstarter campaign; the Canadian science-fiction comedy, *[James vs. His Future Self](/wiki/James_vs._His_Future_Self "James vs. His Future Self")*, starring Daniel Stern, Jonas Chernick, Cleopatra Coleman and Frances Conroy; the South Korean zombie comedy, *[The Odd Family: Zombie on Sale](/wiki/The_Odd_Family:_Zombie_On_Sale "The Odd Family: Zombie On Sale")*; the bonkers insanity of, *Mutant Blast* (acquired and distributed via our friends with [Troma Entertainment](/wiki/Troma_Entertainment "Troma Entertainment")); the Australian slasher, *The Furies*; *Making Monsters*; *[Paradise Hills](/wiki/Paradise_Hills_(film) "Paradise Hills (film)")* (starring Emma Roberts, Awkwafina, Eiza González, and Milla Jovovich); the World Premiere of the Canadian action film, *Contracts*; the South African supernatural thriller, *8* (which has since been retitled as, *[The Soul Collector](/wiki/The_Soul_Collector_(2019_film) "The Soul Collector (2019 film)")*); *Homewrecker*; the exorcism thriller, *The Assent*; the horror anthology, *[The Mortuary Collection](/wiki/The_Mortuary_Collection "The Mortuary Collection")*, starring Clancy Brown; the Polish WWII thriller, *Werewolf (Wilkolak)*; the World Premiere of the Canadian science-fiction, *Enhanced*; the dark comedy, *[Come to Daddy](/wiki/Come_to_Daddy_(film) "Come to Daddy (film)")*, starring Elijah Wood and Stephen McHattie; and, [The Wretched](/wiki/The_Wretched_(film) "The Wretched (film)") (as the Closing Night Gala film), directed by Brett and Drew Pierce ([Deadheads](/wiki/Deadheads_(film) "Deadheads (film)")).

Official Selections for the Canadian Shorts After Dark program, included seven films from around the country [2019 Canadian Shorts](https://torontoafterdark.com/2019-can-shorts-after-dark): *Alaska*, *Best Friends Forever*, *Imagine A World*, *Moment*, *No One Will Ever Believe You*, *Plainsong*, and *Zombies And Indians*.

An additional eighteen Canadian short films were selected to screen before each feature film during the festival [2019 Canadian Pre-Feature Shorts](https://torontoafterdark.com/2019-can-shorts-after-dark). Official Selections included, *We Three Queens*, *Eyes Open*, *Far Horizon*, *Make Me A Sandwich*, *Ocimokw! (Go Away!)*, *Grave Sight*, *Down The Rabbit Hole*, *Hearth*, *Barbara-Anne*, *The Lightsaber Maker*, *Kakashat*, *A Noise That Carries*, *Schism*, *Peel*, *Eilid And Damh*, *Dark Before Dawn: Convoy*, *Patterns*, and *The Changeling*.

Official Selections for the International Shorts After Dark program, included eight films from around the world [2019 International Shorts](https://torontoafterdark.com/2019-intl-shorts-after-dark): *Bar Fight*, *Eject*, *Maggie May*, *Place*, *Puzzle*, *La Noria*, *The Haunted Swordsman* and *Your Last Day On Earth*. Additionally, billed as a "Sci-Fi Mini-Showcase", three additional international short films; *Flip*, *Furnace*, and, *Turbo Killer*, were screened with the feature film, *Blood Machines*.

### 2020–2021

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto_After_Dark_Film_Festival&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: 2020–2021")]

Due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Toronto "COVID-19 pandemic in Toronto"), the 15th Edition of the festival, which was scheduled for October 15–23, 2020, was postponed.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

The 15th Edition of the Toronto After Dark Film Festival took place October 13–17, 2021. For the first time, it was organized be an all-virtual event called, Toronto After Dark At Home, with all 12 official selections available to watch right across Canada, powered by the Eventive platform - a [Video on Demand](/wiki/Video_on_demand "Video on demand") (VOD) streaming service.

### 2022

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto_After_Dark_Film_Festival&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: 2022")]

The 16th Edition of the Toronto After Dark Film Festival took place October 19–23, 2022.

### 2023

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto_After_Dark_Film_Festival&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: 2023")]

The 17th Edition of the Toronto After Dark Film Festival took place October 18–22, 2023.

### 2024–2025

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toronto_After_Dark_Film_Festival&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: 2024–2025")]

Because of health issues, Adam Lopez, the festival's founder and director, postponed the 18th Edition from 2024 to 2025. He stepped down as director, and in 2025, died after battling cancer.[[1]](#cite_note-Lopez-1)

The 18th Edition of the Toronto After Dark Film Festival took place October 15–19, 2025.[[1]](#cite_note-Lopez-1)
